original_abstract:
Today, HTTP-based adaptive streaming (HAS) is the dominant paradigm for Internet video delivery. HAS leverages two key features: first, by operating over HTTP/TCP, it utilizes network-friendly transport protocols to achieve seamless firewall/NAT traversal and fair bandwidth sharing. Second, by storing video in discrete bitrate levels, it enables scalable adaptivity without requiring real-time encoding. A common assumption in HAS design is that a client’s observed TCP throughput serves as an accurate proxy for available network capacity and can thus guide optimal video bitrate selection.

We argue that this assumption fails when HAS traffic constitutes a significant portion of Internet data. We demonstrate that when multiple HAS clients compete at a network bottleneck, the interplay between competing flows and discrete bitrate levels prevents clients from accurately perceiving their fair share of bandwidth. Through theoretical analysis and testbed experiments, we show that this limitation leads to persistent video rate oscillations, which severely degrade the user experience.

To address this, we propose that HAS adaptation logic must incorporate an application-layer "probe-and-adapt" mechanism—a strategy conceptually similar to, yet independent of, transport-layer congestion control. We introduce PANDA (Probe AND Adapt), a client-side algorithm that embodies this principle. Our results indicate that PANDA reduces video rate instability by up to 75% compared to conventional commercial algorithms, while maintaining high bandwidth utilization and minimizing the risk of buffer underruns.
humanized_abstract:
HTTP-based adaptive streaming (HAS) is still the mainstream technology for Internet video transmission. The two characteristics of HAS are that the media files are delivered over HTTP/TCP in a network-friendly way, which enables smooth firewall/NAT traversal and fair link bandwidth sharing. The media files are also segmented into different bitrate segments, which achieves the fast adaptability without real-time encoding. Many works in the literature assume that the received TCP throughput on the client-link is the actual network capacity that can be used for selecting the optimal bitrate for playback.

We argue that this assumption is invalid when HAS traffic is a large fraction of the overall Internet traffic. We show that when many HAS clients compete for resources at a bottleneck link, competing effects of flows, combined with the discrete nature of video bitrates, preclude any client from approximating the fair share of the link bandwidth, resulting in stable oscillations of the video playback rates. We confirm this analysis through both simulations and experiments on a large-scale testbed.

We argue that the reason for such HAS rate instability is lack of application layer control over the rate and timing of received packets, and that an application-layer “probe-and-adapt” mechanism must be included in the HAS adaptation logic, in the form of a transport-layer-independent strategy similar in concept to but distinct from congestion control. We name this client-side strategy PANDA, Probe AND Adapt. Using simulations, we demonstrate that PANDA can reduce video rate instability by up to 75% while still achieving a high throughput and a low risk of buffer underruns, as compared to commercial state-of-the-art solutions.

We argue that this assumption is invalid when HAS traffic is a large fraction of the overall Internet traffic. We show that when many HAS clients compete for resources at a bottleneck link, competing effects of flows, combined with the discrete nature of video bitrates, preclude any client from approximating the fair share of the link bandwidth, resulting in stable oscillations of the video playback rates. We argue that the reason for such HAS rate instability is lack of application layer control over the rate and timing of received packets, and that an application-layer “probe-and-adapt” mechanism must be included in the HAS adaptation logic, in the form of a transport-layer-independent strategy similar in concept to but distinct from congestion control. document.input:
Today, HTTP-based adaptive streaming (HAS) is the dominant paradigm for Internet video delivery. HAS leverages two key features: first, by operating over HTTP/TCP, it utilizes network-friendly transport protocols to achieve seamless firewall/NAT traversal and fair bandwidth sharing. Second, by storing video in discrete bitrate levels, it enables scalable adaptivity without requiring real-time encoding. A common assumption in HAS design is that a client’s observed TCP throughput serves as an accurate proxy for available network capacity and can thus guide optimal video bitrate selection.

We argue that this assumption fails when HAS traffic constitutes a significant portion of Internet data. To address this, we propose that HAS adaptation logic must incorporate an application-layer "probe-and-adapt" mechanism—a strategy conceptually similar to, yet independent of, transport-layer congestion control. We introduce PANDA (Probe AND Adapt), a client-side algorithm that embodies this principle. Our results indicate that PANDA reduces video rate instability by up to 75% compared to conventional commercial algorithms, while maintaining high bandwidth utilization and minimizing the risk of buffer underruns.
